coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric detection of H2O2 by peroxidase-like catalyst iron-based nanoparticles synthesized by using hyperaccumulator plant-derived metal solution
coredata.dc:creator: Uzunoğlu, Deniz
Özer, Ayla
coredata.dc:description: H2O2, a by-product of many enzymatic reactions, plays an important role in various fields such as the food industry, cosmetics, textile, electronics, mining, paper, medicine, environmental chemistry, biological analysis, and clinical diagnosis; so, the quantitative detection of it is of great importance. In this work, the iron-based nanoparticles (FeNPs), as a peroxidase-like catalyst for the colorimetric detection of H2O2, were synthesized by using the acidic leaching solution of an iron hyperaccumulator plant, Pinus brutia. The experimental results proved that FeNPs exhibited strong intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and could efficiently catalyze the oxidation of chromogenic substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2. The catalytic reaction by FeNPs was in accordance with typical Michaelis–Menten kinetics, which showed that FeNPs possessed a much higher affinity to H2O2 and TMB than horseradish peroxidase (HRP). FeNPs were utilized as a peroxidase-like catalyst for the detection of H2O2 in a relatively wide range of 0.05–0.20 mM with a lower detection limit of 0.05773 μM. In brief, this work reported the first use of iron-based nanoparticles synthesized from a hyperaccumulator plant in the sensitive, selective, and reliable detection of H2O2, which may promote the development of new peroxidase-like nanocatalysts for colorimetric detection applications.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric detection of H2O2 by peroxidase-like catalyst iron-based nanoparticles synthesized by using hyperaccumulator plant-derived metal solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering Department, Mersin University, Çiftlikköy Campus, Mersin, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Engineering Department, Mersin University, Çiftlikköy Campus, Mersin, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: H2O2, a by-product of many enzymatic reactions, plays an important role in various fields such as the food industry, cosmetics, textile, electronics, mining, paper, medicine, environmental chemistry, biological analysis, and clinical diagnosis; so, the quantitative detection of it is of great importance. In this work, the iron-based nanoparticles (FeNPs), as a peroxidase-like catalyst for the colorimetric detection of H2O2, were synthesized by using the acidic leaching solution of an iron hyperaccumulator plant, Pinus brutia. The experimental results proved that FeNPs exhibited strong intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and could efficiently catalyze the oxidation of chromogenic substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2. The catalytic reaction by FeNPs was in accordance with typical Michaelis–Menten kinetics, which showed that FeNPs possessed a much higher affinity to H2O2 and TMB than horseradish peroxidase (HRP). FeNPs were utilized as a peroxidase-like catalyst for the detection of H2O2 in a relatively wide range of 0.05–0.20 mM with a lower detection limit of 0.05773 μM. In brief, this work reported the first use of iron-based nanoparticles synthesized from a hyperaccumulator plant in the sensitive, selective, and reliable detection of H2O2, which may promote the development of new peroxidase-like nanocatalysts for colorimetric detection applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection of H2O2 could be performed by many analytical techniques such as titrimetric, colorimetric, fluorometric, chemiluminescence, and electrochemical methods [1]. Among these methods, the most common approach to the detection of H2O2 is the oxidation of some substrates with H2O2 in presence of a peroxidase enzyme (usually horseradish peroxidase-HRP) to produce substances with colored, fluorescent, or electroactive properties. While HRP has advantages such as high substrate specificity and yield under mild conditions, its catalytic activity is highly sensitive to harsh environmental conditions (strong acidic and basic pHs high temperature) and it is easily denatured or digested by proteases, which inevitably restrict its practical application. In addition, the preparation, purification, and storage of natural enzymes like HRP are generally time-consuming and expensive [2]. In recent years, effective peroxidase enzyme-mimetics have been sought in order to circumvent the stability and cost problems of biological catalysts like HRP. In this respect, enzyme-mimetic nanomaterials stand out as a new candidate of potential efficient materials for colorimetric detection of H2O2 due to low cost, high stability, easy preparation and applicability, controllable structure and composition, and adjustable catalytic activity. It is stated that enzyme-mimetic nanomaterials can compete with biological enzymes like HRP used in conventional detection methods. However, especially in the field of biological analysis and clinical diagnosis, the research on the detection of H2O2 at low concentrations is still continuing nowadays. There are many studies showing that excessive H2O2 production in the body causes pathological changes such as DNA damage, aging, and neurodegenerative diseases; also, it may be closely related to the formation of diseases such as malignant tumors, Parkinson, and Alzheimer [3–9]. However, for elucidating the relationship between H2O2 and these diseases, some difficulties are encountered with the sensitive detection of H2O2 in complex biological media. For this reason, studies on enhancing the catalytic activity, sensitivity, selectivity, and stability of enzyme-mimetic nanomaterials are still being attracted attention in order to improve/develop the issue, which limits the application of the H2O2 detection in complicated biological media. It has been discovered that Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles [10], Prussian blue nanoparticles [2], Co3O4-reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite [11], magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles [12], silver nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots [13], manganese selenide nanoparticles [14], nanoparticles of porphyrin functionalized ceria [15], Fe(III)-based coordination polymer nanoparticles [16], copper oxide-gold nanoalloys [17], palladium nanoparticles modified electrospun CoFe2O4 nanotubes [18], CeO2/Zeolite nanocomposite [19], Cu2+-modified hollow carbon nanospheres [20], CuO nanoparticle decorated UiO‑66 [21], carbon silver nano-assembly [22] have intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity on classic peroxidase substrates in the presence of H2O2. The literature survey shows that the nanoparticles used in the studies of colorimetric H2O2 detection are synthesized by chemical methods and these nanoparticles are mostly used more effectively in composite forms. In these studies, both the synthesis of nanoparticles by chemical methods and the additional process of forming composites are factors that increase the cost of colorimetric detection. In this context, it is thought that the non-composite nanoparticle-based colorimetric detection method developed in this study will be crucial in overcoming the drawbacks. Herein; an easy-to-apply, stable, sensitive, selective, and low-cost colorimetric detection method evaluating non-composite FeNPs synthesized with the new synthesis technique using a hyperaccumulator plant as a natural metal salt source has been aimed to develop for the colorimetric detection of H2O2. With the developed nanoparticle synthesis method, besides reducing the cost of nanoparticle synthesis, it is also possible to convert a natural resource, Pinus brutia grown in the vicinity of the chromite (FeCr2O4) stratum, into economic value. The detection of H2O2 is evaluated by measuring absorbance values of the colored oxidation products resulting from the oxidation reaction of TMB with FeNPs in presence of H2O2. Within this scope, the enzymatic kinetics and mechanism as well as the sensitivity, selectivity, and stability of FeNPs were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterisation of iron-based nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Iron-based nanoparticles (FeNPs) were synthesized via a new method developed by our team, using a hyperaccumulator plant of Pinus brutia as the metal source and NaBH4 as the reductant agent. Basically, the acidic leach solution prepared from P. brutia was mixed with NaBH4 under the desired experimental conditions, and FeNPs were synthesized upon the chemical reduction principle. The details of the synthesis method and the results of some characterization studies were given in our previous work [23]. In addition to the characterization studies that have been done, some additional analyses were carried out with Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), Thermogravimetric Analyzer (TGA), Differential Thermogravimetric Analyzer (DTA), and Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) in order to elucidate the structure of the nanoparticles in details. Also, nitrogen adsorption–desorption measurements were performed to determine Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area and porosity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of peroxidase-like catalytic activity of FeNPs for colorimetric detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), a widely used peroxidase substrate, in the presence of H2O2, was tested for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with the synthesized FeNPs and was compared with the results of the commercial HRP. For this purpose; three UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvettes were prepared to contain 500 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH = 2.0), 500 μL of 100 mM H2O2 solution, and 250 μL of 2.0 mM TMB solution. And then, 100 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution was added to the first cuvette, 100 μL of 0.1 mg/L HRP solution to the second cuvette, and 100 μL of distilled water to the third cuvette. After that, the spectrums of the solutions containing the oxidation products of TMB were recorded with UV–vis spectrophotometer at 300–1100 nm wavelength range. After spectrum scanning, the absorbance values of the solutions containing the oxidation products of TMB were recorded with UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength [24]. The effect of environmental conditions such as pH (2.0–14), acetate buffer:TMB volume ratio (1:1–1:4), temperature (20–75 ⁰C), H2O2 concentration (0.001–1000 mM), FeNPs concentration (100–900 μL≈0.1–0.9 g/L) [and HRP concentration (100–900 μL≈0.01–0.09 mg/L)] on the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs and HRP was investigated by one-factor-at-a-time method, and the optimum environmental conditions for these colorimetric detection systems were determined.

In order to prove the mechanism of the colorimetric detection of H2O2, it was investigated whether the hydroxyl radicals (OH•) were formed in the colorimetric detection media. For this purpose, a fluorometric method was used in which terephthalic acid was used as the agent. According to the method, the terephthalic acid (TPA) and the formed OH• radicals react to form 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid product with the fluorescent property. In order to detect the fluorescent product, 400 μL of the different concentrations of H2O2 solutions (0.05–0.50 M), the different concentration equivalents of volumes of FeNPs solutions (100–400 μL≈0.025–0.10 g/L), and 2800 μL of the acetate buffer (pH=4.0) solutions were mixed into UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvettes. Then, 400 μL of 5.0 mM terephthalic acid solution was added to each run. The experimental planning was given in Table 1. The spectrums of runs with the following compositions were performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer at 315 nm excitation wavelength and 335–615 nm emission wavelength range [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic studies for colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs and HRP
ce:section.ce:para:
Steady-state kinetic analysis was performed to determine the catalytic properties of FeNPs and HRP in the colorimetric detection systems where TMB and H2O2 were used as substrates individually. In experimental studies conducted for this purpose, the kinetic data were obtained by keeping one substrate concentration constant while the other was changed. The experimental runs were presented for the catalytic properties of FeNPs and HRP in
Tables 2 and 3 at constant TMB concentration and H2O2 concentration, respectively. The absorbance values of the solutions, the compositions of which were given in Tables 2 and 3, containing the oxidation products of TMB recorded at predetermined time intervals with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of minimum limit of detection of FeNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric and quantitative detection of H2O2 can be done based on the oxidation of TMB in the presence of FeNPs. For this purpose, 500 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH = 2.0), 500 μL of H2O2 solution at different concentrations (0.001–1.0 mM), and 100 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution were added sequentially into 250 μL of 2.0 mM TMB solution in UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvette. The absorbance values of the solutions containing the oxidation products of TMB were recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength. Accordingly, a calibration line was formed by plotting the different concentrations of H2O2 against the absorbance values at these concentrations. The limit of detection (LOD= 3σ/s) was calculated according to the signal which is equivalent to 3 times the standard deviation of the blanks [26].

It is desired that the catalyst used in detection systems can be used for a long time without losing its activity. Accordingly, 500 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH=2.0), 500 μL of 100 mM H2O2 solution, and 100 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution were added sequentially into 250 μL of 2.0 mM TMB solution in UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvette. The absorbance value of the solution containing the oxidation products of TMB was recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength. In order to determine the stability of FeNPs in the colorimetric detection of H2O2, this procedure was repeated for 20 months.

It is stated in the literature that titanium-containing substances such as C4K2O9Ti .2 H2O, TiOSO4 can be used as chromogenic agents in the colorimetric detection of H2O2. For this reason, the applicability of the method in this study was demonstrated by statistically comparing a proven method with the method developed in this study. For this purpose, in the presence of FeNPs and C4K2O9Ti .2 H2O separately, colorimetric H2O2 detection was performed as follows:
i.
The experimental study for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs was carried out by preparing five different sets. First, five different solutions at the same concentration (0.05 mM H2O2) were prepared from the 500 mM stock solution of H2O2. Then, 500 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH=2.0), 500 μL of each 0.05 mM H2O2 solution, and 100 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution were added sequentially into 250 μL of 2.0 mM TMB solution in UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvette. The experiments were repeated for five different H2O2 solutions prepared. The absorbance values of the solutions were recorded with UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength. The concentration values of H2O2 were calculated from the obtained calibration line equation.
ii.
In order to apply the colorimetric H2O2 detection method with C4K2O9Ti .2 H2O, a calibration line was created first according to the titanium oxalate method [27]. Then, the colorimetric detection method was applied in the same way for five different solutions at the same concentration (5.0 mM H2O2) prepared from the 500 mM stock solution of H2O2, and absorbance values were recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 390 nm wavelength. The concentration values of H2O2 were calculated from the obtained calibration line equation.

It is stated in the literature that there have been some difficulties in the sensitive monitoring of H2O2 in complex biological media. Considering this situation, the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs was performed in the presence of different components that can be found in biological media such as NaCl, KCl, CuCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2, Al(NO3)3, (NH4)2HPO4, glucose, lactose, maltose, fructose, sucrose, urea, uric acid, ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, lactic acid, L-cysteine, glutathione, dopamine, and melamine. For this purpose, in the presence of the stated different components (each component: 1000 μL 1.0 mM), 500 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH=2.0), 500 μL of 0.1 mM H2O2 solution, and 100 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution were added sequentially into 250 μL of 0.5 mM TMB solution in a UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvette. The absorbance value of the solution containing the oxidation products of TMB was recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength [28,29].

The recovery values were calculated with Eq. (1) given below:

where, Ai is the absorbance value in the presence of the component i, Ar is the absorbance value in the presence of reference that is distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the effect of other components except for iron ion in the leach solution prepared from P. brutia on the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the effect of other components except for iron ion in the leach solution prepared from P. brutia on the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs, the synthetic solutions were prepared from various iron ion sources such as FeSO4.7 H2O and Fe(NO3)3.9 H2O, and FeNPs were synthesized by using these solutions. For this purpose, 0.70 g of NaBH4 was added to 20 mL of the synthetic solutions into the 50 mL-Erlenmeyer flasks containing 13.81 mg/L of iron ions at 1.18 pH and 65 °C temperature. The dark black mixtures containing nanoparticles obtained as a result of the synthesis were magnetically stirred at 1000 rpm for 30 min. Then, the mixtures were centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 5.0 min. FeNPs separated from the supernatant were washed with pure ethanol [30]. Then, the obtained FeNPs were dissolved in pure ethanol and the solutions were prepared at a concentration of 1.0 g/L. After that, the colorimetric H2O2 detection experiments were performed with FeNPs synthesized with the iron ion-containing solutions prepared from three different iron sources (leach solution prepared from P. brutia, FeSO4.7 H2O, and Fe(NO3)3.9 H2O). For this purpose, 500 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH=2.0), 500 μL of 100 mM H2O2 solution, and 100 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution were added sequentially into 250 μL of 2.0 mM TMB solution in UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvette. The absorbance value of the solution containing the oxidation products of TMB was recorded with UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength [24].

In our previous work [23], the characterization results showed that the crystal phases of Fe3O4, FeOOH, γ-FeOOH, and Fe0 were observed in the XRD pattern of FeNPs. It was determined from SEM images that FeNPs were in spherical-like shapes; and also, the average particle size of FeNPs was determined to be 82.19 nm. EDX analysis showed that FeNPs had the elements of O, Fe, Ca, Na, Mg, and Cl in descending order.

In this study, the nitrogen adsorption–desorption measurements were performed to determine BET specific surface area and porosity. The obtained BET isotherm for FeNPs was presented in Fig. 1(a). According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) nomenclature, the obtained isotherm in Fig. 1(a) exhibited type III isotherm with type H3 hysteresis. This type of isotherm represents the adsorption in non-porous or macroporous solids that the adsorbent-adsorbed forces are quite weak [31]. In this case, it was necessary to examine the pore structure. For this purpose, Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore size distribution analysis was performed. The adsorption and desorption average pore widths of FeNPs were 76.16 nm and 57.15 nm, respectively. The fact that BJH adsorption and desorption average pore widths greater than 50 nm confirmed the type III isotherm obtained in this study. The BET surface area of FeNPs was determined as 32.67 m2/g. The BET surface area of FeNPs was compared with the iron-containing nanoparticles in the literature and the results were summarized in Table 4. According to the iron-containing nanoparticles in the literature, the BET surface area of FeNPs synthesized in this study was found to be at an average level.

The thermal behavior of the synthesized FeNPs was investigated by DSC, TGA/DTA. The mass change and heat flow curves with temperature increase obtained by DSC and TGA/DTA thermal analyzes of FeNPs were presented in Fig. 1(b). A sharp peak at 32 °C and a broad band in the 70–120 °C range with a center of approximately 95 °C were detected in the DTA curve given in Fig. 1(b). These were due to the removal of water physically adsorbed onto the FeNPs surface. It was determined in the TGA curve that the percentage of mass loss was about 2.49% in the first step in the temperature range of 0–130 °C, due to the removal of physically adsorbed water from the structure of FeNPs [32,33]. While the DSC curve gave a peak indicating an endothermic reaction at approximately 196 °C, the DTA curve indicated a large amount of mass loss at approximately this temperature. It was seen in the TGA curve that this mass loss continued up to 430 °C. The reason for this mass loss was thought to be resulting from the degradation of the components other than iron detected by EDX analysis in the FeNPs structure. It was determined that the percentage of mass loss was about 10.45% in the second step in the temperature range of 130–430 °C due to the degradation of the components other than iron originating from the hyperaccumulator plant structure used during the synthesis of FeNPs [34,35]. It was observed that the mass loss due to the degradation of the components originating from the plant structure continued after 430 °C. A wide band was detected in the DTA curve in the temperature range of 800–1000 °C, which was thought to be caused by the conversion of the Fe3O4 phase in the FeNPs structure to the Fe2O3 phase. It was determined that the percentage of mass loss was about 4.96% in the third step in the temperature range of 430–1000 °C due to the continuation of the degradation of the components originating from the plant structure used during the synthesis of FeNPs and the conversion of the Fe3O4 phase in the FeNPs structure to the Fe2O3 phase [36,37].

TEM images at different magnifications were given in Fig. 2(a) and (b). The TEM images indicated that FeNPs were spherical-like in shape and their diameters ranged from 10 to 100 nm. When the sphere-like structures were examined closely, it was seen that FeNPs had a core-shell structure. Accordingly, the dark-appearing core part of the core-shell structure of FeNPs was zero-valent iron (Fe0) with a large atomic number, and the lighter-colored shell part was iron oxide/hydroxides (Fe3O4, FeOOH, γ-FeOOH) with a smaller average atomic number than Fe0 [30,55,56].

The surface elemental composition of FeNPs was analyzed by using XPS in the region of 0–1300 eV. XPS spectrums of FeNPs and high-resolution Fe 2p were presented in Fig. 3(a) and (b), respectively. It was observed from Fig. 3(a) that FeNPs contained the peaks belonging to the orbitals of Mg 1 s at 1305.15 eV, Na 1 s at 1072.16 eV, Fe 2p at 711 eV and 725 eV, O 1 s at 532.25 eV, Ca 2 s at 439.22 eV, Ca 2p at 348.30 eV. The peak of O 1 s at 532.25 eV could be deconvoluted into three components at 530.7, 531.6, and 532.25 eV corresponding to crystal O2− in metal oxides, O atoms in metal hydroxides or hydroxyl groups, and weakly adsorbed species, respectively [57]. Additionally, it was observed that there were the Auger peaks of O element between 950 and 1000 eV, Na element at 500 eV, and Mg element at 306.36 eV, which was caused by the emission of another electron due to the transition made by electrons in the inner shell of an atom as a result of the scattering of one or more electrons or a photon from an external source (Auger effect) [58,59]. According to Fig. 3(b), the peaks of Fe 2p at 711 eV and 725 eV could be deconvoluted into five components at 702.5, 711.4, 720.7, 725.1, and 735.4 eV corresponding to the zero valent iron (Fe0), 2p3/2 orbital, 2p3/2 satellite peak, 2p1/2 orbital, and 2p1/2 satellite peak, respectively. The spin orbit peak of Fe 2p3/2 at 711.4 eV was deconvoluted into the peaks at 711.2 and 713.5 eV related to the components of Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively while the spin orbit peak of 2p1/2 at 725.1 eV was deconvoluted into the peaks at 724.4 and 727.9 eV related to the components of Fe2+ and Fe3+, respectively. Also, the main peaks of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 included the satellite peaks on the higher binding-energy side at 720.7 eV and 735.4 eV, respectively, which could be due to different species of oxidized iron [60–62].

As a result, the structure of FeNPs synthesized from a hyperaccumulator plant, P. brutia was elucidated by various characterization analyses in this study and it was confirmed that the properties of FeNPs were similar to iron-containing nanoparticles synthesized using synthetic metal salt in the literature. Therefore, it was concluded that the prepared leach solution of P. brutia could be unwaveringly used instead of the synthetic iron salt solutions to synthesize iron-based nanoparticles by reduction principle, which may promote the evaluation of different hyperaccumulator plants for the synthesis of various nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of peroxidase-like catalytic activity of FeNPs for colorimetric detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance spectrums of oxidized TMB catalyzed by FeNPs, HRP, and distilled water for control were given in Fig. 4(a). It was concluded that FeNPs catalyzed the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2, increasing the absorbance values of diimine at 450 nm and oxidized TMB (oxTMB) at 655 nm. Also, it was determined that HRP caused a much less increase in the absorbance value of oxTMB at 655 nm compared to FeNPs; however, it was thought that this difference resulted from the difference between the concentrations of HRP (0.1 mg/L) and FeNPs (1.0 g/L). The concentrations of HRP and FeNPs could not be studied equally because HRP is an expensive enzyme, which prevents studying at a concentration as high as 1.0 g/L for HRP. It was concluded that FeNPs like HRP could also effectively catalyze the oxidation of TMB.

According to the prescribed steps of the colorimetric H2O2 detection with FeNPs (Fig. 4(b)); H2O2 decomposes in the presence of FeNPs with the Fenton-like reaction, forming OH• radicals, and these radicals cause the oxidation of the substrate TMB. As a result of the reaction of OH• radicals with the substrate TMB; TMB loses electrons to form a cation radical first, then the reaction ends with the formation of yellow diimine and blue-colored oxidized TMB (oxTMB: charge transfer complex) [63]. These reaction steps are quite similar to the oxidation of TMB substrate as a result of the decomposition of H2O2 with HRP, a peroxidase enzyme, in the literature. Therefore, it was determined that FeNPs could be evaluated as a peroxidase-like catalyst in the colorimetric detection of H2O2.

In the colorimetric H2O2 detection with FeNPs, a fluorometric method in which terephthalic acid (TPA) was used as the agent was used to evidence the prescribed detection steps given in Fig. 4(b) by proving the formation of OH• radicals. The spectrum scanning at 315 nm excitation wavelength and 335–615 nm emission wavelength range was performed to detect the fluorescent product in the prepared nine different mixtures given in Table 1 with a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Since RUNs 1 and 2 had no peak in the 335–615 nm emission wavelength range, their spectrums were not presented. The fluorescence spectrums obtained for the different H2O2 concentrations (Table 1, RUNs 3–7) and the concentration of FeNPs (Table 1, RUNs 5, 8, and 9) were presented in Fig. 5(a) and (b), respectively.

In all of the spectrums presented in Fig. 5(a) and (b), the maximum peak was obtained at 440 nm belonging to the fluorescent 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid product, which formed as a result of the reaction of TPA and OH• radicals formed in the reaction media. Accordingly, the prescribed steps given in Fig. 4(b) were confirmed to be valid for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs [25]. In addition, it was determined from Fig. 5(a) and (b) that the fluorescence intensities at 440 nm wavelength increased with increasing the H2O2 concentration (at constant FeNPs concentration) and the FeNPs concentration (at constant H2O2 concentration). According to this; when the H2O2 concentration increased in the presence of sufficient FeNPs in the detection media (Fig. 5(a)), the fluorescence intensity increased due to the increase in OH• radicals formed as a result of the decomposition of H2O2. Similarly; in the case of sufficient H2O2 in the detection media, as FeNPs concentrations increased, the decomposition of H2O2 increased and the amount of OH• radicals formed accordingly increased, thus more intense peaks were obtained at 440 nm (Fig. 5(b)).

In order to investigate the pH effect on the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs [or HRP], 500 μL of acetate buffer solution at desired pH value, 500 μL of 100 mM H2O2 solution, and 100 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution [or 100 μL of 0.1 mg/L HRP solution] were added sequentially into 250 μL of 2.0 mM TMB solution in UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvette. Experimental studies for the different pH values (the pH range of 2.0–14) were repeated and the absorbance values of the solutions containing the oxidation products of TMB were recorded with UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength. It was observed from Fig. 6(a) that the activity of FeNPs decreased as the pH value increased, while the activity of HRP increased with increasing pH from 2.0 to 5.0, and then decreased. It is known that HRP is effective in weak acidic environments; therefore, it was determined that HRP showed the best activity at pH= 5.0 and the basic conditions reduced its activity [64]. The optimum pH was determined as 2.0 in the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs, and its activity was found to be considerably reduced in the basic conditions. It was concluded that the acidic conditions were more efficient rather than basic conditions for the detection of H2O2 with FeNPs, as the number of active sites on FeNPs surface decreased as a result of the adsorption of OH─ ions in the media under basic pHs to the surface of FeNPs and the formation of Fe—OH bonds.

For the investigation of the buffer:TMB volume ratio effect on the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs [or HRP], by adjusting the volume ratios of acetate buffer and TMB as the total volume of 750 μL, the desired volume of acetate buffer solution (pH=2.0), 500 μL of 100 mM H2O2 solution, and 100 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution [or 100 μL of 0.1 mg/L HRP solution] were added sequentially into the desired volume of 2.0 mM TMB solution in UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvette. The absorbance values of the solutions containing the oxidation products of TMB were recorded with UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength. According to Fig. 6(b), the maximum activity of FeNPs was obtained at the 2:1 acetate buffer:TMB (v:v) ratio. This situation could be explained by the pH value and the amount of TMB. At 4:1, 2:1, 3:2, 1:1, 2:3, 1:2 and 1:4 acetate buffer:TMB (v:v) ratios, the pH values of the media were 1.88, 2.02, 2.11, 2.26, 2.42, 2.58, and 2.71, respectively. FeNPs showed low activity at 4:1 acetate buffer:TMB (v:v) ratio because the pH value required for TMB oxidation could not be achieved (pH=1.88) and also the amount of TMB was low at this ratio. It was determined that the activity of FeNPs decreased as the acetate buffer:TMB (v:v) ratio decreased from 2:1–1:4 because the increased pH value, as well as the excess amount of TMB reduced the activity of FeNPs. As a result, the optimum acetate buffer:TMB (v:v) ratio was determined as 2:1 for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs. In the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with HRP; it was determined that the absorbance values increased due to the increase in the concentration of the oxidation product oxTMB as the acetate buffer:TMB volume ratio decreased and accordingly as the amount of TMB and pH of the media increased [65].

The effect of the catalyst concentration on the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs [and HRP] was investigated by varying in the range of 0–900 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs [or 0.1 mg/L HRP]. As in many studies in the literature [64,66,67], the studied concentration of HRP was lower than that of FeNPs because the preparation, purification, and storage of the natural enzyme of HRP are time-consuming and expensive, causing HRP to be studied at low concentrations. For the investigation of the effect of the catalyst concentration, 500 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH=2.0), 500 μL of 100 mM H2O2 solution, and the different concentration volumes of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution [or 0.1 mg/L HRP solution] (the total volumes of the catalyst solutions were adjusted to 1000 μL with ethanol for FeNPs and distilled water for HRP) were added sequentially into 250 μL of 2.0 mM TMB solution in UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvette. The absorbance values of the solutions containing the oxidation products of TMB were recorded with UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength. According to Fig. 6(c), the absorbance values increased with increasing the amount of FeNPs up to 700 μL (≈ 0.7 g/L), then remained approximately constant. It was determined that the concentration of the oxidation product oxTMB increased due to the increase in the formed OH• radicals, and therefore the absorbance values increased as the amounts of FeNPs increased. For the FeNPs volumes greater than 700 μL (≈ 0.7 g/L), since the H2O2 concentration was constant, no more OH• radicals formed and no more TMB oxidation took place with the increase of FeNPs amount. As a result, the optimum FeNPs volume was determined as 700 μL (≈ 0.7 g/L) for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs. For the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with HRP, the absorbance values increased with an increase in the amount of HRP up to 500 μL (≈ 0.05 mg/L), then decreased. It was related to the increase in the oxidation of TMB as the amount of HRP increased. In the presence of more than 500 μL (≈ 0.05 mg/L) of HRP in the colorimetric detection media, it was observed that the oxidation of TMB occurred very rapidly and the blue-colored oxTMB product rapidly turned to the yellow-colored diimine by-product and so, the light-colored by-product caused the solution color to lighten, resulting in the decrease in the absorbance values.

The effect of temperature on the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs [and HRP] was investigated in the range of 20–75 ⁰C. For this purpose, 500 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH=2.0), 500 μL of 100 mM H2O2 solution were added sequentially into 250 μL of 2.0 mM TMB solution in UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvette. The prepared solutions were placed in a water bath and heated to the desired temperature. Then, 100 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution [or 100 μL of 0.1 mg/L HRP solution] was added to the solution at the desired temperature. As seen in Fig. 6(d), while the activity of FeNPs was high in the temperature range of 20–45 ⁰C, high activity could be obtained for HRP at the only temperature of 35 ⁰C. Since FeNPs have an inorganic structure, FeNPs are more resistant to high temperatures compared to organic HRP, and this allows the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs in a wide temperature range [68].

In order to investigate the effect of H2O2 concentration on the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs [and HRP], 500 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH=2.0), 500 μL H2O2 solution at different concentrations, and 100 μL of 1.0 g/L FeNPs solution [or 100 μL of 0.1 mg/L HRP solution] were added sequentially into 250 μL of 2.0 mM TMB solution in UV–vis spectrophotometer cuvette. The absorbance values of the solutions containing the oxidation products of TMB were recorded with UV–vis spectrophotometer at 655 nm wavelength. It was determined from Fig. 7(a) and (b) that the absorbance values for both FeNPs and HRP amount increased up to the H2O2 concentration of 1.0 mM, then decreased, and this decrease was greater when HRP was used. The reason for the increase in the absorbance values as the concentration of H2O2 increased up to 1.0 mM was the increase in the concentration of the oxidation product oxTMB due to the increasing the formed OH• radicals. When the concentration of H2O2 was more than 1.0 mM, the excess H2O2 in the media reacted with the OH• radicals formed as a result of the decomposition reaction of H2O2 and formed the less active HO2• radical compared to the OH• radical (H2O2 + OH• → HO2• + H2O) [69]. In this case, it was observed that the concentration of the oxidation product oxTMB decreased due to the decrease in the radical concentration required for the oxidation of TMB, and so the absorbance values decreased at the H2O2 concentrations higher than 1.0 mM. A similar situation existed in the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with HRP. Accordingly, when the H2O2 concentration was higher than 1.0 mM, the excess H2O2 concentration reduced the activity of the HRP, causing a decrease in the absorbance values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis of colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs and HRP
ce:section.ce:para: Since it was concluded that FeNPs could be evaluated as a peroxidase-like catalyst in the colorimetric detection of H2O2, the steady-state kinetic analysis was performed to determine the catalytic properties of FeNPs and HRP. Accordingly, the experimental kinetic data obtained by varying the concentration of a substrate (H2O2 or TMB) while maintaining the remaining conditions unchanged were applied to the double reciprocal of the Michaelis−Menten equation.

Michaelis-Menten equation was given in Eq. (2).
(2)

where, υo is initial reaction rate (M/s), [S] is substrate concentration (mM), Km is Michaelis-Menten constant (mM), and υmax is maximum reaction rate (M/s). Accordingly, the concentrations calculated from the absorbance values obtained at different substrate concentrations were plotted against time and the initial reaction rates (υo) were calculated from the slopes of the tangents drawn at t = 0 to the curves obtained at different substrate concentrations. Then, according to Eq. (3); Lineweaver – Burk plots (1/[So] vs 1/υo) were obtained, and Km and υmax values were calculated from the slope and intercept values of the obtained lines [70].

The concentration of the oxidation product oxTMB was calculated by Lambert-Beer Law equation of C=A/(I.ε), where A is the concentration of the oxidation product analyzed at 655 nm wavelength (M), I is the optical path length (1.0 cm), and ε is the molar absorption coefficient which equivalents to 39,000 M-1.cm-1. The Michaelis-Menten curves obtained for the different concentrations of both TMB and H2O2 were depicted in Fig. 8(a) and (b) for FeNPs and Fig. 9(a) and (b) for HRP. The corresponding double reciprocal plots (Lineweaver-Burk plots) were drawn as shown inset Figs. 8 and 9. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and the maximum reaction velocity (υmax) were calculated from the slope and y-intercept of the Lineweaver-Burk plots. The calculated in this work and the reported in the literature Km and υmax values were summarized in Table 5. Accordingly, Km values obtained using TMB and H2O2 as the substrates for FeNPs were much lower than that of HRP, suggesting that FeNPs had a higher affinity towards TMB and H2O2 as the substrates compared with that for HRP and some other peroxidase-like catalysts listed in Table 5. Since υmax values obtained using TMB and H2O2 as the substrates for FeNPs were higher than that of HRP, it was determined that the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs was faster than that of HRP [71]. It was concluded that FeNPs synthesized from the hyperaccumulator plant, a renewable source, could be used as effectively as the peroxidase-like catalysts synthesized from synthetic metal sources in the literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of minimum limit of detection of FeNPs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine the LOD value of FeNPs for the colorimetric detection of H2O2, a calibration line was formed by plotting the different concentrations of H2O2 against the absorbance values of the oxidation product oxTMB at these concentrations. The obtained calibration line was given in Fig. 10 (a), revealing the good linear relationship between the absorbance and the concentration of H2O2 in the range of 0.05–0.2 mM with a detection limit of 0.05773 μM. The comparison of linear range and LOD for H2O2 detection of this work with the other reported peroxidase-like catalyst in the literature was summarized in Table 6. It was seen that the linear range and LOD of FeNPs were comparable to the peroxidase-like catalysts synthesized from synthetic metal sources in the literature.

The absorbance values of the oxidation product oxTMB recorded for 20 months were presented in Fig. 10.(b). As seen in Fig. 10.(b), within 6 months after the synthesis of FeNPs, there was a slight decrease (∼5.0%) in its activity, the absorbance values decreased dramatically after the 6th month and the decrease in the absorbance values remained approximately constant after the 13th month. As a result, it was concluded that the FeNPs synthesized in this study could be used effectively for at least 6 months in the colorimetric detection of H2O2.

A calibration line with the equation of A390 nm= 0.0749xCH2O2+ 0.0003 (R2 =0.9952) of the method using C4K2O9Ti .2 H2O was formed in the range of 0.5–5.0 mM H2O2 concentration. Then, the concentration values of five different H2O2 solutions at the same concentration (0.05 mM H2O2) prepared from 500 mM stock solution of H2O2 were calculated by using the calibration lines of the methods using FeNPs and C4K2O9Ti .2 H2O; the calculated concentration values were given in Table 7.

The experimental concentration values were compared with each other and the t- and F-tests were used for comparing the population means and variances, respectively. Since the standard deviation of the two experimental groups was not very different from each other, the sampled standard deviation of the two experimental groups was found by Eq. (4) given below:

In the given Eqs. 3–5; S is a sampled standard deviation, s1 and s2 are standard deviations of the two compared experimental groups, n1 and n2 are sample numbers of the two compared experimental groups, n1 +n2–2 is a degree of freedom, X1 and X2 are arithmetic means of the compared experimental groups [65,81].

The proposed colorimetric method was validated against the method using C4K2O9Ti .2 H2O, and the results of statistical analysis were tabulated in Table 8. The results were analyzed by t- and F-tests with confidence levels of 99.5% and 99%, respectively in order to compare the population means and variances. According to Table 8, ttable and Ftable values were greater than t- and F- tests results, respectively; therefore, it was concluded that there were no considerable differences between the compared methods. This result showed that the colorimetric detection method using FeNPs synthesized from the hyperaccumulator plant P. brutia could be reliably applied as the conventional H2O2 detection methods.

In order to determine the selectivity of FeNPs, the colorimetric detection experiments were carried out in presence of the potential interferents that can be found in complex biological media, such as NaCl, KCl, CuCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2, Al(NO3)3, (NH4)2HPO4, glucose, lactose, maltose, fructose, sucrose, urea, uric acid, ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, lactic acid, L-cysteine, glutathione, dopamine, and melamine. From Fig. 11 (a), it was determined that uric acid, ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, L-glutathione, L-cysteine, dopamine, and melamine components interfered with the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs. Since the recovery values given in Table 9 for the components except for uric acid, ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, L-glutathione, L-cysteine, dopamine, and melamine were in an acceptable range ( ± 5.40%), it was concluded that FeNPs could be used effectively in the colorimetric detection of H2O2 in the presence of NaCl, KCl, CuCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2, Al(NO3)3, (NH4)2HPO4, glucose, lactose, maltose, fructose, sucrose, urea, lactic acid components. Since it was determined that ascorbic acid made the most interaction with the method, it could be suggested that the detection of ascorbic acid could be carried out based on the decrease in absorbance as a result of adding it to the media of the colorimetric H2O2 detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the effect of other components except for iron ion in the leach solution prepared from P. brutia on the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance values of the oxidation product oxTMB formed as a result of the oxidation of TMB with FeNPs synthesized from P. brutia, FeSO4.7 H2O, and Fe(NO3)3.9 H2O were given in Fig. 11 (b). The results showed that other components except for iron such as Ca, Mg, K, Na, Al, Ni, Zn, Cu in the leach solution did not interact with the colorimetric detection of H2O2, suggesting that the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs was independent of the iron source used in the nanoparticle synthesis. It was concluded that FeNPs synthesized with the leach solution prepared from P. brutia could be used as an environmentally friendly, low-cost, and renewable peroxidase-like catalyst in the colorimetric detection of H2O2 instead of FeNPs synthesized using synthetic iron salt solutions in the literature.

In this study, FeNPs were successfully synthesized via a new method, using a hyperaccumulator plant instead of synthetic metal salt, developed by our team and found to be exhibited inherent peroxidase-like activity in the colorimetric detection of H2O2 for the first time. The nature of the peroxidase-like activity of FeNPs was attributed to the formation of OH• radicals as a result of H2O2 decomposition with the Fenton-like reaction. Like HRP and the reported nanoparticle-based peroxidase mimetics, the catalytic activity of FeNPs was dependent on pH, buffer: TMB volume ratio, catalyst amount, temperature, and H2O2 concentration. The experimental data of the oxidation of TMB by FeNPs in presence of H2O2 well fitted to Michaelis–Menten steady-state kinetics and it was observed that FeNPs had a good affinity to H2O2 and TMB substrates. The detection limit of H2O2 could be achieved as low as 0.05773 μM and a wider concentration range of H2O2, demonstrating high sensitivity and good reliability for applications in real samples. The peroxidase-like activity of FeNPs was over 95% after storage for 6 months and FeNPs possessed good selectivity in presence of various interferents that can be found in real samples. The accuracy of the colorimetric detection of H2O2 with FeNPs was validated by the proven colorimetric method using C4K2O9Ti .2 H2O. The experimental findings demonstrated that the colorimetric detection of H2O2 could be efficiently carried out with FeNPs synthesized from a renewable source independent of expensive instruments and complicated sample pretreatments.

Deniz UZUNOĞLU: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ayla ÖZER: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
